title: Loops the Easy Way: ISZ
lesson:
On the Baby, looping meant managing the instruction counter by hand and testing the accumulator's sign. The PDP-8 gives you proper tools. DCA ('Deposit and Clear Accumulator') stores AC into memory and clears it, which is the natural way to save a result. And ISZ ('Increment and Skip if Zero') adds one to a memory word and skips the next instruction if the result wraps around to zero.

That one instruction is the classic PDP-8 loop counter. Load a counter with the negative of your iteration count (say, negative 5), and ISZ it each pass. It climbs from negative 5 toward zero, and exactly when it hits zero, ISZ skips the JMP that would repeat the loop. You'll see this pattern in almost every PDP-8 program ever written.

The deeper point: real memory plus a few well-chosen instructions turn looping from a puzzle into a habit. That's a big part of what made the minicomputer practical for everyday work.
prompt: Use an ISZ loop to count from 0 up to 5, leaving 5 in the accumulator. Keep a counter that starts at negative 5, and a running SUM that you bump up with ISZ each pass.
starterCode:
*200
        CLA CLL
        TAD MFIVE   / AC = -5
        DCA CTR     / CTR = -5
        DCA SUM     / SUM = 0
LOOP,   ISZ SUM     / SUM = SUM + 1
        ISZ CTR     / CTR = CTR + 1, skips the JMP when CTR hits 0
        JMP LOOP
        TAD SUM     / AC = SUM (which is 5)
        HLT
SUM,    0
CTR,    0
MFIVE,  -5
hints: A counter that starts at -5 reaches 0 after exactly 5 ISZ steps.
ISZ CTR skips the NEXT instruction when CTR becomes 0, so put JMP LOOP right after it and the loop ends there.
ISZ SUM bumps your running total each pass, and TAD SUM at the end loads it into AC.
successText: AC must equal 5, counted up by an ISZ loop.
reference:
*200
 CLA CLL
 TAD MFIVE
 DCA CTR
 DCA SUM
LOOP, ISZ SUM
 ISZ CTR
 JMP LOOP
 TAD SUM
 HLT
SUM, 0
CTR, 0
MFIVE, -5
